title: The communication complexity of distributed subgraph detection
speaker: Rotem Oshman
abstract: In distributed systems, communication between the participants in the computation is usually the most expensive part of the computation. Theoretical models of distributed systems usually reflect this by neglecting the cost of local computation, and charging only for messages sent between the participants; in particular, we usually assume that the computation proceeds in rounds, and in each round each participant can send only a limited number of bits.

proof_stages.label: Communication as the resource and communication-complexity viewpoint
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates distributed computing as a setting where local computation is free and communication is costly, then recalls two-party communication complexity and explains why its bit lower bounds are useful but not identical to distributed round lower bounds.

proof_stages.label: Shared-blackboard subgraph-detection model and hard source problems
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines the broadcast blackboard model with one graph vertex per player, b bits per player per round, adjacent-edge input sharing, and fixed-subgraph detection, then identifies set disjointness and gap Hamming distance as the communication problems used for reductions.

proof_stages.label: Two-party reductions for cycle detection
proof_stages.summary: A two-party simulation template encodes disjointness instances into possible cycles, yielding a tight n/b lower bound for C5 detection, an approximation lower bound via gap Hamming distance, and—after replacing a complete bipartite core by a C4-free extremal graph—a tight Turán-scale lower bound for C4 detection.

proof_stages.label: Number-on-forehead reduction for triangles
proof_stages.summary: Because two-party black-box reductions cannot capture triangle detection under edge input sharing, the argument switches to three-party number-on-forehead disjointness and a tripartite graph with many edge-disjoint triangles to obtain a near-tight deterministic triangle lower bound while leaving the randomized case open.

proof_stages.label: Turán-number peeling upper bound for general subgraphs
proof_stages.summary: Using the fact that an H-free graph has at most ex(n,H) edges, the algorithm repeatedly has many low-degree vertices reveal all incident edges and recurses, giving an upper bound of Turán-number-over-nb up to logarithmic factors and matching the known cycle cases.

proof_stages.label: Congested-clique unicast model and circuit lower-bound barrier
proof_stages.summary: For the model where each node may send different b-bit messages to different neighbors, the talk shows that strong lower bounds would imply new bounded-depth circuit wire lower bounds by simulating ACC/related circuits layer-by-layer with balanced gate assignments and short summaries for heavy gates.

proof_stages.label: Open problems and broader distributed lower-bound directions
proof_stages.summary: The talk closes by emphasizing open lower-bound constructions for general subgraphs, true graph-property testing questions such as connectivity, and the harder setting where communication occurs over an arbitrary network graph rather than a complete graph.

examples.name: Complete bipartite graph as an odd-cycle-free Turán example
examples.note: The complete bipartite graph is cited to make the Turán number concrete for triangles and other odd cycles, explaining why the upper bound specializes to order n/b there.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker reasons concretely about complete bipartite gadgets, noting that they create four-cycle rectangles inside one player’s side and later invoking their Turán behavior for odd cycles.

examples.name: Fast congested-clique tasks: sorting, MST, and triangle detection
examples.note: Known fast algorithms for sorting, minimum spanning tree, and triangle detection illustrate how much more powerful the unicast congested-clique model is than the broadcast model.
